title: Troubleshoot User Authorization Issue
description: Step-by-step process to diagnose and resolve a user access or authorization problem.

steps.action: Capture the failed authorization check
steps.reason: SU53 shows exactly which authorization object, field, and value was missing at the time of the last failed check. This is your primary diagnostic tool — always start here.

steps.action: Review the user's assigned roles and profiles
steps.reason: Confirm which roles are currently assigned to the user and whether the expected role is present and active for the correct validity period.

steps.action: Open the relevant role and check the authorization data
steps.reason: In the Authorization tab of PFCG, search for the missing authorization object from SU53. Check if the field value is maintained correctly.

steps.action: Add or correct the authorization and generate the profile
steps.reason: After editing the authorization, you must regenerate the authorization profile — otherwise the change has no effect on the user's actual access.

steps.action: Re-assign or refresh the role on the user if needed
steps.reason: In most cases profile regeneration is enough. If the role was newly added, confirm the assignment is saved correctly in SU01.

steps.action: Ask the user to re-test the failing action
steps.reason: Confirm the fix resolved the issue. If SU53 still shows a failure, repeat the process for any additional missing authorization objects.

title: Investigate Missing Transport Change
description: How to investigate why a transport was imported successfully but the expected change is not visible in the target system.

steps.action: Check the transport import log in STMS
steps.reason: Navigate to the import queue for the target system and open the import log for the transport. Look for warnings or errors — a green status icon can be misleading if there were partial errors.

steps.action: Verify the correct client and system were targeted
steps.reason: Transports are client-specific for Customizing requests. Confirm the import was done for the right client number in the right system.

steps.action: Open the transport request and check its objects
steps.reason: In SE09, display the transport request and expand the object list. Confirm the expected object (table entry, program, etc.) is actually included in the request.

steps.action: Check if another transport overwrote the object
steps.reason: If multiple transports contain the same object, the last one imported wins. Look at the transport history for the object to detect overwrite conflicts.

steps.action: Re-import the transport if necessary
steps.reason: If the import had errors or the wrong client was used, re-import the transport from the STMS import queue using the correct settings.

title: Fix an OData Service Not Found Error
description: Diagnose and resolve a 404 or service unavailable error on an SAP Gateway OData service.

steps.action: Search for the service and check its activation status
steps.reason: The most common cause is that the service is not activated on the Gateway. Search by service name and confirm it shows as active. If missing, add it.

steps.action: Verify the ICF service node is active
steps.reason: Even if activated in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, the underlying ICF HTTP service node must also be active in SICF. Navigate to the OData service path and check its status.

steps.action: Clear the backend metadata cache
steps.reason: Stale metadata cache on the backend can cause the Gateway to fail to retrieve service metadata. Run this on the backend system first.

steps.action: Clear the Gateway frontend metadata cache
steps.reason: After clearing the backend cache, also clear the Gateway hub cache to ensure fresh metadata is served to consumers.

steps.action: Verify the service definition is complete and generated
steps.reason: If the service was recently modified, ensure the runtime artifacts were regenerated in SEGW using Generate → Runtime Objects. Without regeneration, changes are not active.

steps.action: Test the service URL in a browser or tool
steps.reason: Access the service document URL (e.g., /sap/opu/odata/svc/SERVICE_NAME/) to confirm it returns a valid XML metadata response.

title: Create and Set Up a New SAP User
description: Complete process for creating a new SAP user, assigning roles, and verifying access.

steps.action: Create the user with logon data and personal info
steps.reason: Set the username, first/last name, email, and initial password. Choose the correct user type (Dialog, Service, Communication, etc.) based on the use case.

steps.action: Assign the required roles on the Roles tab
steps.reason: Roles carry the authorization profiles. Assign all roles the user needs and set appropriate validity dates. Avoid using manual profiles — use roles.

steps.action: Confirm roles have up-to-date authorization profiles
steps.reason: If the roles show a yellow or red status, the authorization profile needs to be regenerated. A role without a current profile grants no authorizations.

steps.action: Ask the user to log on and test key transactions
steps.reason: Have the user attempt their key tasks. If they hit authorization errors, use SU53 immediately after the failure to capture the missing object.

steps.action: Review any access failures and adjust roles
steps.reason: Use SU53 output to fine-tune role authorizations in PFCG. Repeat until all required access is confirmed.

title: Build and Activate a New OData Service
description: End-to-end flow for developing, registering, and testing a new OData service in SAP Gateway.

steps.action: Create or open the OData project and define the data model
steps.reason: Define entity types, properties, and associations that represent your data model. This is the foundation of the service.

steps.action: Implement the DPC (Data Provider Class) methods
steps.reason: The generated DPC_EXT class must be extended with ABAP code to handle reads, creates, updates, and deletes for each entity set.

steps.action: Generate runtime objects
steps.reason: Use Generate → Runtime Objects in SEGW to create the backend registration and activate the service definition. This must be done after any model change.

steps.action: Register the service on the Gateway hub
steps.reason: Add the new service in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE to make it accessible via the Gateway URL. Set the system alias pointing to the backend.

steps.action: Activate the ICF node if not already active
steps.reason: Ensure the HTTP service node for the OData service path is active in SICF, otherwise the Gateway will return HTTP 403 or 404.

steps.action: Use the SAP Gateway Client to test the service
steps.reason: Within /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, use the Gateway Client button to call the service $metadata and entity sets directly, confirming correct responses before handing off.
